content:
{
  "title": "Fact-Checking Chain-of-Thought: On-the-Fly Veracity Self-Verification in Prompting",
  "problem": "Large language models frequently hallucinate factual content despite instruction tuning and RLHF. Existing prompting methods insufficiently enforce real-time verification of generated claims.",
  "motivation": "As LLMs are deployed in high-stakes contexts, hallucination risks erode trust and utility. Prompting methods today rarely encourage explicit, stepwise fact-checking during inference—missing an opportunity to exploit LLM reasoning abilities for self-correction. Novel prompting could achieve human-level factual accuracy without post-hoc external verification.",
  "method": "We propose Fact-Checking Chain-of-Thought (FC-CoT) prompting: after each factual assertion in the model's response, the prompt instructs the model to (a) generate a brief justification or evidence snippet, (b) explicitly rate its own confidence in the claim, and (c) state the source (real or imagined) underlying the assertion. This chain is interleaved with the answer generation, not appended post-hoc. The method exploits LLMs' generative capabilities for justification and self-assessment, reducing hallucination by fostering metacognitive evaluation during output production.",
  "experiment_plan": "FC-CoT will be evaluated on TruthfulQA, FactCC, and HotpotQA, with comparisons to vanilla prompting, regular Chain-of-Thought, and retrieval-augmented prompting. Metrics include factual accuracy, hallucination rate (via human and automated judgments), and response length. Ablations will quantify the impact of evidence, confidence, and source-attribution components."
}
